skill.name: mobile-ios-design
skill.description: Guides native iOS interface design with Apple Human Interface Guidelines, SwiftUI layout patterns, navigation models, accessibility, and system components.
skill.summary: Design polished iOS apps with Apple HIG guidance and practical SwiftUI patterns.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported code execution, weak crypto, credential, reconnaissance, network, and critical heuristic signals. Review found the execution and sensitive-data signals are false positives from Markdown Swift examples and iOS terminology, with no prompt injection or executable scripts found. The only remaining concern is benign hardcoded documentation and sample URLs, so the skill is low risk and safe to publish.

low_findings.title: Markdown Swift Examples Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reported external command locations are Markdown Swift code fences and inline Swift examples. No executable script, shell invocation, Ruby backtick execution, or command construction was found in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are documentation code fences or Swift UI samples. The file tree contains only Markdown files, and no runnable shell or Ruby code was found.

low_findings.title: Swift Identifiers Misclassified as Cryptographic or Reconnaissance Risk
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance alerts match normal SwiftUI and iOS design terms such as Hashable, Data, id, destructive buttons, and URL routing. These examples do not implement cryptography, collect system information, or access host files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed context shows UI navigation and component examples, not security-sensitive primitives. No hashing algorithm, encryption code, system probing command, or credential access appears at these locations.

low_findings.title: Credential File Alert Dismissed as Collection Key Usage
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The sensitive certificate or key alert points to a Swift dictionary key used while rendering grouped list items. No private key, certificate file, secret value, token, or credential material was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious word is a local variable named key inside a Swift ForEach loop. The surrounding code is a UI list example and contains no credential file content.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation and Sample URLs
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE_LOW: The skill includes hardcoded external URLs in resource links and a sample privacy-policy link. They do not transmit data or run network code, but marketplace reviewers should know the skill references external Apple documentation and an example domain.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are plainly visible and are used as documentation or sample links. There is no evidence of data exfiltration, automatic fetching, or credential transmission.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Dismissed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The critical heuristic depended on code execution, network, and credential-access alerts. After review, code execution and credential access were false positives, and the network evidence is limited to benign links.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The underlying static signals were reviewed in context and do not form a dangerous behavior chain. No prompt injection, obfuscation, script execution, or data transfer behavior was found.

content.value_statement: iOS teams need interfaces that feel native, accessible, and consistent with Apple platform behavior. This skill turns Apple HIG and SwiftUI patterns into practical design and implementation guidance.
actual_capabilities: Applies Apple Human Interface Guidelines principles to iOS app screens and flows.
Recommends SwiftUI layout patterns using stacks, grids, lists, forms, and materials.
Guides native navigation with NavigationStack, TabView, sheets, deep links, and coordinators.
Improves accessibility with Dynamic Type, VoiceOver labels, semantic colors, and safe-area behavior.
Provides reusable component patterns for buttons, menus, dialogs, loading states, and empty states.
Adapts designs for iPhone, iPad, Dark Mode, SF Symbols, and platform conventions.
limitations: It provides design and implementation guidance, not automated UI testing or App Store review approval.
SwiftUI examples need adaptation to each app model, data layer, and deployment target.
It does not replace current Apple documentation for newly released APIs.
It does not create production visual assets, icons, or brand systems by itself.

use_cases.title: Plan a New iOS Feature Flow
use_cases.description: Map screens, navigation, system components, and accessibility requirements before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Convert Mockups into SwiftUI Patterns
use_cases.description: Choose native SwiftUI structures for lists, forms, buttons, sheets, and adaptive layouts.

use_cases.title: Audit an Existing Mobile Interface
use_cases.description: Review a screen for HIG alignment, Dynamic Type support, Dark Mode behavior, and navigation clarity.

prompt_templates.title: Review One Screen
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner audit of a single iOS screen.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this iOS screen against Apple HIG. Focus on hierarchy, touch targets, Dynamic Type, Dark Mode, and accessibility.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Native Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Plan a feature before detailed design.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a native iOS flow for [feature]. Include navigation structure, key screens, system components, empty states, and error states.

prompt_templates.title: Translate Mockup to SwiftUI
prompt_templates.prompt: Translate this mockup into SwiftUI implementation guidance. Recommend layouts, controls, navigation, state handling, and accessibility details.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Complex App Experience
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review across multiple screens and devices.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this iOS app experience for HIG alignment across iPhone and iPad. Identify navigation risks, accessibility gaps, component inconsistencies, and SwiftUI refactor opportunities.

output_examples.input: A settings screen with account fields, notification controls, and a privacy link.
output: Recommends a grouped Form with clear Account, Preferences, and About sections.
Uses native Toggle and Picker controls instead of custom controls for common settings.
Adds semantic labels, Dynamic Type support, and a standard external privacy link row.

output_examples.input: A product browsing flow that opens detail screens and supports search.
output: Suggests NavigationStack with typed destinations for product detail screens.
Adds searchable lists, empty states, loading states, and retry actions.
Defines iPad adaptations with split layouts and preserved navigation state.

output_examples.input: An existing SwiftUI screen that feels visually inconsistent with iOS.
output: Flags hardcoded colors, fixed text sizes, unclear hierarchy, and oversized custom controls.
Recommends semantic colors, SF Symbols, standard spacing, and accessible button roles.
Prioritizes fixes by user impact and implementation effort.

best_practices: Start with Apple system components before designing custom controls.
Test every recommendation against Dynamic Type, VoiceOver, Dark Mode, and safe areas.
State the target iOS version and device classes when asking for SwiftUI guidance.
anti_patterns: Do not force web or Android interaction patterns into native iOS flows.
Do not use fixed font sizes, hardcoded colors, or cramped touch targets.
Do not request App Store compliance guarantees without separate policy and technical review.

faq.question: Does this skill write SwiftUI code?
faq.answer: It can provide SwiftUI patterns and implementation guidance. Generated examples still need project-specific integration and testing.

faq.question: Can it review existing iOS screens?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide screenshots, mockups, or screen descriptions and ask for HIG, accessibility, and SwiftUI feedback.

faq.question: Does it support iPad design?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for adaptive layouts, larger screens, multitasking, and navigation differences on iPadOS.

faq.question: Can it guarantee App Store approval?
faq.answer: No. It helps with interface quality and platform conventions, but App Store approval requires separate review.

faq.question: What inputs work best?
faq.answer: Use a clear feature goal, target devices, iOS version, screenshots, user tasks, and known constraints.

faq.question: Is this only for designers?
faq.answer: No. Designers, iOS engineers, product managers, and design reviewers can use it for practical iOS interface decisions.
